Hattie and Mum look at each other. Mum says "I thought something had happened to you." Her voice cracks. Hattie feels it in her chest. She doesn't know what to say. Bea's mum arrives. Bea leaves. Hattie is alone with Mum. Mum says they'll talk in the morning. It is the morning. They both know that. **Hook**: Caught. **Cliffhanger**: Alone with Mum, the conversation that hasn't happened yet. ## Chapter 8: "Monday" (~1000 words) Monday. Hattie on the bus to school, stomach tight. She expects whispers, phones, the video everywhere. She gets to form room. Nothing. Priya says "good weekend?" like normal. Hattie finds Bea at her locker. Bea looks tired but composed. They walk to assembly. Someone—Jake, irrelevant—does a weak impression of being sick, laughing. Bea looks at him. He stops. They sit together. Assembly is about litter. Hattie thinks about the night—the shed, the running, the kitchen light. She thinks about how nothing has changed and everything has. At lunch, they sit in the canteen. Bea eats chips. Hattie has the packed lunch Mum made—Mum has made it every day since Thursday, with a note, which is Mum's way of not talking about it. Bea asks for a crisp. Hattie gives her one. They don't talk about the video. They don't talk about the shed. Bea says "Geography mock's Friday." Hattie says "I haven't revised." Bea says "same." They eat. Hattie's phone buzzes. A text from Bea, who is sitting across from her: "thanks." Hattie looks up. Bea isn't looking at her. Hattie puts her phone away. The canteen is loud. They sit there. **Hook**: Will the video destroy them? **Resolution**: The world didn't end. It's just Monday. They're just sitting here.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Hattie Crowther ## Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws Hattie is 14 and lives with her mum, Sarah, and her 8-year-old brother, Ollie, in a semi-detached house on a street where everyone has the same brown fence. Her dad left two years ago—moved to Sheffield with a woman from his office, sends birthday cards with £20 and a generic message. Hattie copes by never thinking about him. She's in Year 10, predicted mostly 6s and 7s, except in Geography where she's predicted a 5 and her teacher keeps suggesting "intervention sessions" which is apparently not as medical as it sounds. She works Saturdays in a charity shop because her mum made her get a job to "build character." She has a talent for spotting vintage labels and resells them on Vinted at a markup. Hattie's humour is her primary tool. She deploys it to defuse awkwardness, to hide fear, and to avoid dealing with emotions directly. When she's genuinely frightened, the humour stops and she gets very literal and quiet. She has a tendency to catastrophise in lists ("Reasons this is fine: 1. 2. There is no 2"). She says "like" too much when nervous and hates that she does. She bites her thumbnails until they bleed, particularly during exams and horror films. She is chronically online but pretends she isn't—her screen time is genuinely alarming. **Flaws**: She can be passively resentful, stewing in feelings of inadequacy rather than addressing them. She sometimes enjoys being the "together" one in friendships because it makes her feel superior, which she knows is unkind. She is terrible at asking for help and views needing it as failure. She can be mean internally about people in ways she'd never express. **Examples/Anchors**: - She watches true crime documentaries with a notebook, "just in case." - Her charity shop is a Cancer Research UK where the manager, Dawn, calls everyone "babe" and smells faintly of Olbas Oil. - She has a crush on a boy named Cal in her form who doesn't know she exists; she has constructed an entire narrative about him based on his pencil case. - She revises by highlighting everything in yellow until the page glows. - She worries her chest is too flat in a way she can't articulate, especially around Bea, who developed early. ## Physicality Mousy brown hair she scrapes back in a bobble that's always stretching out. Narrow shoulders, perpetually cold hands. She wears the same three hoodies in rotation. She has a scar on her left knee from falling off a bike at 9—the dad was still there then. She carries herself slightly hunched, trying to be smaller than she is. When she listens, she tilts her head; when she lies, she over-explains. Her school shoes are scuffed at the toes from dragging her feet. ## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships Hattie's primary allegiance is to Bea—they've been friends since Year 7 when Bea lent her a pad in an emergency and they bonded over how awful the school toilets are. Hattie sometimes feels like Bea's supporting cast, the "funny friend" rather than the "interesting friend." She loves Bea fiercely but also resents how easy Bea finds social situations. Hattie's motive on this night is to help Bea, but it's also to prove she's the kind of person who shows up. Hattie's relationship with her mum is loving but strained by mutual inability to talk about feelings. Mum shows love through routine: clean uniform, signed forms, packed lunches. Hattie wishes her mum would ask how she is; her mum wishes Hattie would tell her. Ollie is 8 and obsessed with Minecraft; Hattie finds him annoying but would defend him to the death. Her dad is a non-topic. When someone mentions Sheffield, she changes the subject. ## Voice / Dialogue Quotes **Internal monologue (anxious):** "Right. Okay. She's fine. She's probably fine. People text emojis at midnight all the time. That's a thing people do. Except Bea doesn't, because Bea's phone is dead, which means this is someone else's phone, which means she needed to borrow a phone, which means—" **Internal monologue (scared, losing humour):** "I don't know where I am. I don't know where I am and my phone's on 12% and there's a fox looking at me from behind a wheelie bin and I think that's the most frightened I've been of anything tonight, actually, the fox." **Dialogue (to Bea, trying to minimise):** "It's fine. It's fine, Bea. It's like—one video. People post stuff all the time. By Monday no one'll even remember. Monday's ages away." **Dialogue (to her mum, caught):** "I was just—we were just—it's not as bad as it looks. I know what it looks like. It's not that." --- # Bea Folorunsho ## Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws Bea is 14, same year as Hattie. She's the youngest of three—her sisters are 19 and 22, both at university, both high achievers. Her parents are Nigerian-British, strict but loving; her mum is a nurse, her dad works in IT. The household runs on expectations: her sisters set the bar, Bea is expected to clear it. She feels this pressure constantly but expresses it only sideways, through rebellion rather than conversation. Bea is magnetic. She walks into a room and people adjust toward her. She's funny, but not in Hattie's dry way—she's loud, physical, willing to make a fool of herself. She's the friend who starts dancing at a party first, who talks to boys first, who suggests things first. This magnetism is both her power and her vulnerability: she draws attention, and attention can turn. She gets male attention, which is a source of social power but also a source of fear she doesn't fully articulate. Older boys notice her. She likes being noticed and hates that she likes it. **Flaws**: She can be thoughtless—she doesn't always clock how her social confidence affects Hattie. She makes plans and assumes Hattie will follow. She's bad at being alone and seeks company compulsively, which is how she ends up at Tom L's party. She apologises by changing the subject, never directly. She holds grudges over small things and forgives big things too easily. **Examples/Anchors**: - She started her period at 12, before anyone else, and managed it like a military operation. The 🩸 emoji code grew from this—initially it meant "emergency supplies needed." - She has 487-day Snapchat streaks with people she doesn't actually like. - She's predicted 8s and 9s but her maths teacher has noticed she's stopped trying. - She watches Hattie's Vinted depop and buys things to support her but never wears them. - She cuts her own fringe when stressed—it always goes wrong. ## Physicality Taller than Hattie, broad-shouldered, carries herself like she's used to being looked at. Dark skin, short box braids that she's always adjusting. Big laugh, big gestures. She wears gold hoops every day despite the school's jewellery policy—she's been told off about them so many times it's become a running joke. When she's upset, she goes very still and very quiet, which is more alarming than her being loud. The missing shoe on the night in question is a Joules trainer she saved up for; she's more upset about the shoe than the video, initially. ## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships Bea's allegiance to Hattie is genuine but complicated by the fact that Bea has other friend groups—the party crowd, the sporty girls—and Hattie doesn't. Bea assumes Hattie will always be there because Hattie always has been. She doesn't realise how invisible Hattie sometimes feels. Bea's motive on the night of the party was to feel older, to be included, to be seen as more than "the youngest Folorunsho sister." The party was supposed to prove she's not a kid anymore. Instead, it proved the opposite. Her relationship with her parents is loving but strained by expectation. Her mum will be furious but also frightened; her dad will go quiet, which is worse. Her sisters will text concerned messages that read as condescending. ## Voice / Dialogue Quotes **Dialogue (on the bench, fragmented):** "I wasn't even—that drunk. I wasn't. I'd had like, three drinks, maybe four, but they were—Tom kept making them and I didn't want to look like—I didn't want to be the Year 10 who couldn't handle it. So I just. Kept going. And then I was in the kitchen and everything was spinning and I just—everything came up. Like, everywhere. And everyone was looking and someone had their phone out and I could hear them laughing and I just ran." **Dialogue (in the shed, lashing out):** "You don't get it. You don't—you weren't there. You don't know what it's like when people are watching you and you can feel them deciding what you are. You're just—Hattie, you're just there. No one's looking at you like that. You don't know." **Dialogue (changing the subject, which is how she apologises):** "Your mum's going to actually kill you. Like, properly. Like, I'll be at your funeral. What flowers do you want? I'll go halfsies with Priya." --- # Sarah Crowther (Mum) ## Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws Sarah is 42, works in admin at a logistics company. She's been doing it for eleven years. She wears fleeces indoors and reads Sally Rooney novels she gets from the library but never finishes. Since her husband left, she's developed a routine that she maintains with grim determination: dinner at 6:30, BBC News at 10, bed by 11. She drinks too much coffee and not enough water. She cries in the car sometimes, where no one can see. She loves her children fiercely but doesn't always know how to show it in ways they can receive. She signs forms, makes lunches, buys uniform before Hattie asks. She asks "how was school?" and gets "fine" and doesn't push because she doesn't know how to push without it becoming an argument. She feels guilty about the divorce even though it wasn't her decision. **Flaws**: She goes silent when she's angry, which is worse than shouting. She says "right" in a tone that means the opposite. She compares Hattie to herself at that age and finds her wanting, but would never say so. She wants Hattie to talk to her but doesn't know how to start the conversation. She's terrified of something happening to her children and covers this with rules and curfews. **Examples/Anchors**: - She always has custard creams in the cupboard "just in case." - She has a group chat with other mums called "School Mums" where they share information about homework and teenage trends; she lurks more than she posts. - She watches Hattie's Vinted account to make sure she's not being scammed. - She reorganises the kitchen cupboards when stressed. ## Physicality Shorter than Hattie, which Hattie secretly enjoys. Brown hair going grey at the temples, dyed at home every six weeks with a box that claims to be "natural warm brown." She wears reading glasses on her head and can never find them. She moves efficiently—she's been running a household alone for two years and it shows in the economy of her movements. When she's frightened, she goes very still and her mouth goes flat. ## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships Sarah's primary motive is keeping her children safe. When she discovers Hattie is gone, she feels a terror she hasn't felt since the divorce—the feeling that the ground is disappearing. She's also angry, but the anger comes after the fear, not instead of it. She doesn't know how to express either. Her relationship with Hattie is a quiet stalemate of unexpressed feeling. She's proud of Hattie but worries she's too closed off. She wishes Hattie would talk to her the way she talks to Bea. She's slightly intimidated by her own daughter's sharpness. ## Voice / Dialogue Quotes **Dialogue (when they come home, not shouting):** "Right." [long pause] "Right. Sit down. No—sit down. Don't move. I'm going to—just—sit there." **Dialogue (trying not to cry):** "I thought something had happened to you. I thought—I was sat here and I thought, she's gone. Something's taken her. And I couldn't—the police said to wait an hour and I was sat here and I couldn't—" **Dialogue (the morning after, not talking about it):** "There's a packed lunch on the side. I've put an extra KitKat in. And can you tell Ollie to stop putting his tablet in the bathroom, I've told him three times."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: Incoming The text comes at 12:04am. I know the exact time because I'm staring at my phone anyway, lying on my bed with my Geography textbook open on my chest like it's going to absorb through osmosis. It isn't. I've highlighted everything on the page about coastal erosion in yellow and now it just looks like the textbook is glowing. I don't remember what any of it says. My phone screen is doing that thing where I've been scrolling too long and everything feels slightly unreal, like I'm watching my own life through glass. I'm not doing anything important. I'm looking at Bea's Instagram story from three hours ago—her in the bathroom mirror, lip gloss, the caption "getting ready xo"—and then at a video of a dog wearing a raincoat, and then at nothing in particular, just the feed, the feed, the feed. Then the text. 🩸🩸🩸 I stare at it. Three blood drops. I know what it means because I'm the one who made it up. Or Bea made it up. We made it up together, that night at hers, watching the documentary about the girl who went missing in Cornwall. The documentary with the terrible reenactments—the actress they'd hired kept doing this thing with her hair, tucking it behind her ear, and Bea said "she's doing that because the real girl did that in her last CCTV" and I said "how do you know" and Bea said "I read about it" and we both went quiet because the real girl was dead and we were eating Doritos. We decided we needed a code. Not a normal one—not "call me" or "SOS" because those are boring and also what if someone saw them. A code that meant: I need you. Right now. Not later. Not tomorrow. Now. Bea chose the blood drops because she said it looked serious without being obvious. I said it looked like a period emoji. She said "exactly, no one will question it." We were thirteen. It felt important. I stare at the text. Bea sent it at 12:04am. Her location sharing is off. Her story was posted three hours ago, which means she was getting ready at 9pm, which means she was going out, which means she was going to the party she mentioned in the group chat earlier—"Tom L's, his parents are away, should be fun x"—and I'd said "have fun" because I wasn't invited because Tom L is sixth form and I don't exist to him. I call her. It rings. Rings. Rings. Goes to voicemail. Her voicemail is still the default one because she never changed it. The robot voice says "the person you've called—" and I hang up. I text: "Bea???" Delivered. Not read. I text: "Bea is everything ok" Delivered. Not read. I text Priya: "have you heard from Bea" Nothing. Priya's probably asleep because Priya is a normal person who goes to bed at 10:30. I call Bea again. Voicemail again. The robot voice again. My stomach is doing something. Not the normal anxiety stomach, not the before-a-mock stomach. Something else. I'm aware of the house—mum's door closed, Ollie's nightlight leaking under his door, the boiler clicking off. Everything quiet. Everything fine. Everything fine and Bea's not reading my texts and she sent three blood drops at midnight and I'm lying here in pyjama bottoms with a T-shirt that says "Cancer Research UK" on it because I stole it from the stock room. I sit up. Reasons this is fine: 1. She's probably asleep. 2. She's probably drunk and fell asleep. 3. She's probably fine and sent it by accident. 4. She— There is no four that's fine. I get dressed in the dark. Jeans. Hoodie—the grey one, not the school one. Trainers. I don't look in the mirror because I don't want to know what my face is doing. I take my keys from the hook by the back door, moving slowly because the hook squeaks if you're too fast. I've practised this. Not on purpose—I just mean I've left the house quietly before, to put the bins out, to get something from the car. I know which floorboards creak. The one by the bathroom. The two at the top of the stairs. I avoid them. The kitchen is dark. The dishwasher light is on, that green glow. Mum's mug is on the draining board, rinsed out, ready for tomorrow. There's a note on the fridge: "Hattie—Geography revision!! Don't forget!!" with two exclamation marks, which is Mum's way of being firm without being here. I let myself out the back gate. The latch sticks. I pull it, and it makes a sound like a bird, and I freeze, but nothing happens. No lights. No mum at the window. The cold hits me. It's that specific cold—not winter cold, but the cold you get at midnight in October when you're not dressed for being outside because you didn't plan on being outside. The kind that gets into your ears and your fingertips immediately. I shove my hands in my hoodie pocket and my right hand finds a Quality Street wrapper that's been in there since September. I'm outside. I'm outside and I don't know where Bea is. The street is empty. Everyone's lights are off except the Patels at number 14, who are always awake. Their TV is blue through the curtains. I start walking toward the bus stop, and I don't have a plan, and I don't have a coat, and the Quality Street wrapper is stuck to my fingers. My phone is on 34%.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates genuine craft and a clear authorial voice. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Character**: Hattie's voice is immediately distinctive and consistent with the profile. The numbered list ("Reasons this is fine: 1. 2. 3. 4. There is no four that's fine") is exactly the kind of anxious, self-aware humour the plan called for. The detail about the Cancer Research UK T-shirt stolen from the stock room is perfectly specific and characterful. The Geography textbook absorbing through osmosis, the yellow highlighting—these are precise, lived-in details that feel genuinely teenage rather than performed. The voice doesn't overreach; it stays in its lane. **Execution of Plan**: The chapter follows its plan closely. We get the text, the context of the code (the Cornwall documentary, the terrible reenactments), the failed attempts to reach Bea, the Instagram scrolling, the getting dressed in the dark, the brother's nightlight, Mum's closed door, the back gate, the cold. The cliffhanger—standing outside not knowing where Bea is—lands cleanly. The plan's instruction to let the code's meaning come in fragments is honoured; we get the backstory naturally embedded in Hattie's anxious processing. **Prose Quality**: The prose is clipped and controlled. "The feed, the feed, the feed" is a nice rhythmic choice that captures mindless scrolling without being showy. The description of the cold—"not winter cold, but the cold you get at midnight in October when you're not dressed for being outside because you didn't plan on being outside"—is exactly the kind of specific, unpoetic observation the plan called for. The Quality Street wrapper is a superb detail: mundane, specific, slightly comic, and it recurs at the end to close the chapter with a grounded image rather than a dramatic one. **Tone**: The dark comedy is present but not forced. The Doritos-while-watching-a-documentary-about-a-dead-girl moment is genuinely funny and genuinely uncomfortable in the right proportion. The humour doesn't trivialise; it contextualises. The plan's instruction to avoid overwritten prose is followed—Hattie isn't poetic, she's tired and scared, and the prose reflects this. **Weaknesses**: The chapter is almost entirely setup, which is appropriate for Chapter 1, but it means there's limited dramatic tension beyond the central mystery. The documentary backstory section, while well-executed, is slightly long—the explanation of the code takes a full paragraph and a half, which edges toward the plan's warning about "explaining the code in one neat paragraph." It's not quite a violation, but it's close. The Priya detail ("Priya's probably asleep because Priya is a normal person who goes to bed at 10:30") is a small moment of internal meanness that's consistent with the character profile but feels slightly underused—it's there and then gone. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the cliffhanger energy the plan describes; "standing outside in the dark, realising she doesn't know where Bea actually is" is more of a quiet landing than a cliffhanger. This is a tonal choice that works, but it means the chapter ends on a slightly flat note rather than a propulsive one. **Dialogue**: There's no actual dialogue in this chapter, which is appropriate—Hattie is alone. The "dialogue" is all internal or via text. This is a reasonable choice for Chapter 1. **Metaphor and Poetry**: The "watching my own life through glass" line is the one moment that edges toward the slightly generic, but it's brief and doesn't linger. The textbook-glowing-yellow image is good. No purple prose, no forced metaphors that don't land. **Coherence**: Perfectly coherent. The timeline is clear, the logic of Hattie's decisions is sound, the world is established efficiently. **Overall**: This is a confident, well-executed opening chapter. It does what it needs to do—establish voice, establish stakes, establish character—without overreaching. The prose is controlled, the humour is earned, and the details are specific enough to feel real. It's not a masterpiece, but it's genuinely good YA fiction with a distinctive voice. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: N29 The bus stop is four minutes away if you walk fast and five if you walk normally and about seven if you're me, tonight, because I keep stopping to check my phone like Bea might have texted back in the last forty seconds. She hasn't. The screen is just the time—12:11, 12:13, 12:14—and the three blood drops sitting there in our chat like a thing I can't take back. I rehearse what I'll say if anyone asks. If a bus driver asks: I'm going to my mum's. If a police officer asks: I'm going to my mum's. If anyone asks: I'm going to my mum's. My mum lives here. This doesn't hold up. I keep rehearsing it anyway. The N29 comes at 12:19. I know this because I've looked at the timetable before, not because I've ever actually caught it. The night bus is different from the day bus. For a start, there's no one normal on it. The day bus has school kids and old people and that man who always has a supermarket bag. The night bus has—well. There's a man upstairs eating chips with a kind of focus I've never seen another human being apply to anything. He's not looking at his phone. He's not watching anything. He's just eating chips, one at a time, staring ahead, like the chips are the most important thing that's ever happened to him. Two women in heels are sitting near him, one of them crying about someone called Darren. "He's a prick, babe," the other one keeps saying. "He's a massive prick." The crying woman says "I know" and cries harder. The chip man doesn't look up. I sit at the front because I don't want to be near any of them. The bus driver glances at me in his mirror. I don't know if he's thinking anything. Probably not. Probably I'm just a girl on a bus. My phone buzzes and my whole body jumps but it's just Priya: "sleeping?? what's up?" I text back: "have you heard from Bea tonight" Three dots. Then: "no?? why" I don't reply. I don't know how to explain it. I don't know if I'm right to be worried. I feel like if I say it out loud—type it—it becomes a real thing, and right now it's still just a text I could be overreacting to. I open Instagram. Bea's story is still up—the bathroom mirror, the lip gloss. I tap through her other stories. Party. Music. A shot of a kitchen I don't recognise, bottles on the counter, someone's arm in the frame. I screenshot it and zoom in. There's a window in the background and through it I can see—fences. Back gardens. And a shed. A green shed with a felt roof, the kind everyone has. This means nothing. Everyone's shed looks like that. I find a photo from earlier—Bea with Tom L. Tom L is tall and has that hair that boys have now where it looks like they've just woken up but they've actually spent twenty minutes on it. His arm is around Bea and she's doing a peace sign. She looks happy. She looks like she's having the night she wanted. I text a boy from school, Marcus, who I only speak to when I need something: "do you know where Tom L lives" He takes four minutes to reply. During those four minutes I watch the dark go past the window. We're in the part of town I don't know—the part with the big houses and the cars parked in driveways instead of on the street. Marcus: "idk maybe the ones by the retail park?? his mate lives there" The retail park. I pull the cord. The bus stops and I get off and the cold is worse here because there are no houses, just the retail park—Argos, Pets at Home, a gym that's still lit up inside. Everything is closed and massive and the car park is empty except for one trolley that's somehow ended up by the kerb. Behind the retail park I can see the new-build estate: rows of identical houses, all brown and beige, all with the same pointed roofs. It looks like someone copy-pasted a house and forgot to stop. I stand on the pavement. I don't know which house. I don't know which street. I'm standing here in my hoodie with a Quality Street wrapper in my pocket and 28% battery and I don't know where my best friend is. I call Bea one more time. It rings. And rings. And— "Hattie." Her voice. Small. Shaking. Not Bea's voice at all, or a version of it I've only heard once before, when her granddad died. "Bea. Where are you." "I'm in the park. The one with the swings." "Which park." "You know. The one by—the one we went to after—Hattie, I don't—" She makes a sound. Not crying. Something before crying. "Stay there," I say. I start running. I don't know which park. I don't know where I'm going. But I'm running, and my trainers are slapping the pavement, and the cold is in my lungs, and somewhere behind these houses Bea is on a bench waiting for me to show up.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this piece is a strong execution of the plan's intentions, demonstrating genuine craft in voice, pacing, and tonal control. Let me examine it critically across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Hattie's voice is remarkably well-sustained. The rehearsal of "I'm going to my mum's" — repeated three times with the self-aware collapse "This doesn't hold up. I keep rehearsing it anyway" — is exactly the kind of anxious, circular thinking the plan promised. The listing tendency is present but not overused. The "28% battery" detail is precisely the kind of mundane specificity that grounds the terror. The internal monologue cycles between catastrophising and minimising in a way that feels genuinely adolescent rather than performed. The chip man is a standout detail — "like the chips are the most important thing that's ever happened to him" — this is the kind of dry, specific observation the plan called for. The Darren scene is economical and funny without being laboured. These bus characters exist as texture, not as commentary, which is exactly right. THE PLAN'S EXECUTION The chapter follows its plan closely: bus stop, N29, bus ecosystem, Instagram scrolling, Marcus text, retail park, lost on the pavement, Bea's call. The cliffhanger lands — Bea's voice described as "a version of it I've only heard once before, when her granddad died" is genuinely affecting and earns its emotional weight without sentimentalising. The chapter ends on running, which is kinetically satisfying. The plan specified "the particular cold of 2am" and "the smell of night buses" — the cold is rendered well ("the cold is in my lungs"), though the bus smell is absent. Minor omission. PROSE QUALITY The prose is clean and controlled. The copy-paste house metaphor ("It looks like someone copy-pasted a house and forgot to stop") is teen-specific and unpoetic in exactly the right way — it doesn't reach for beauty, it reaches for accuracy. The Quality Street wrapper in the pocket is a lovely, throwaway detail that does real work in establishing Hattie's ordinariness. The sentence "I don't know if he's thinking anything. Probably not. Probably I'm just a girl on a bus" is quietly excellent — it captures Hattie's self-erasure without announcing it. WEAKNESSES The Instagram investigation section is slightly mechanical — screenshot, zoom, shed, "this means nothing" — it reads a touch procedural. The plan called for Bea "looking wrong" in a photo, but here she looks happy, which is a small deviation that actually works better dramatically (the contrast between the happy photo and the reality is more unsettling), but it's worth noting. The ending is slightly rushed. "I start running. I don't know which park. I don't know where I'm going." The repetition of "I don't know" three times in quick succession is intentional and effective, but the final paragraph tips slightly toward the declarative — "the cold is in my lungs" is the one moment where the prose reaches for something slightly more literary than Hattie's voice strictly warrants. It's a minor transgression. The dialogue with Bea is brief but well-handled — the fragmentation ("the one by—the one we went to after—Hattie, I don't—") feels authentic. The stage direction "Not crying. Something before crying" is precise and restrained. OVERALL ASSESSMENT This is confident, disciplined writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. The voice is consistent, the comedy is earned through specificity rather than construction, and the emotional escalation is handled with genuine skill. The chapter avoids the pitfalls of overwriting while maintaining momentum. It's not a masterpiece — the ending slightly overreaches, and the Instagram section is mechanical — but it's well above average YA fiction and demonstrates real understanding of how to balance dark material with dry humour. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3: The Swings I find her because of the broken swing. The park is behind the estate—you have to go through a gap in the fence by the recycling bins and then across a field that's mostly mud. I know this because Bea and I came here once in Year 8 after a boy called her the n-word and she didn't want to go home yet. We sat on the swings and she didn't cry and I didn't know what to say so I just sat there. That was a different kind of not-crying. This is worse. The broken swing is the one on the left. The chain's been snapped since forever, hanging down like a dead arm. Bea is on the bench next to it, not the swings. She's hugging herself. One shoe. The Joules one, the navy one she saved up for. The other foot is in a sock that's gone brown at the bottom. "Bea." She looks up. Her face is—mascara. Down both cheeks. Her braids are coming loose at the front, bits of hair sticking to her forehead. She's wearing the top from her Instagram story but it's stretched now, pulled sideways, and there's something on the sleeve. Sick. I can smell it from here. I sit down. I don't say "are you okay" because she isn't. I don't say "what happened" because I can see what happened, sort of. I just sit down and she moves into me, not crying, just leaning, and her head is on my shoulder and she smells like sick and perfume and cold air. "Your hand," I say. She lifts it. Her palm has a cut across it, not deep but long, dried blood smeared down to her wrist. She looks at it like she forgot about it. "Fence," she says. "I climbed over a fence." "Has it stopped bleeding?" "I don't know. I think so." I don't have anything to clean it with. I pull my sleeve down over my hand and press it against her palm. She winces but doesn't pull away. We sit like that for a minute. Somewhere behind us a dog is barking, far away, and then it stops. "What happened," I say. Not a question. Just—say it. Bea doesn't move her head from my shoulder. "I was at Tom L's. You know Tom L's?" "You posted about it." "Yeah. It was—it was fine at first. There were sixth formers there, and some people from our year, and I was like—it was fine, Hattie. I was having a good time. Tom kept making drinks and I didn't want to—I didn't want to be the Year 10 who couldn't handle it." "Okay." "So I just. Kept going. And then everything was—" She pulls away from my shoulder, sits up. She's not looking at me. She's looking at the broken swing. "I was in the kitchen and everything was spinning and I just—everything came up. Like, everywhere. On the counter. On the floor. On my shoe. And everyone was—" She stops. "There were people there. People I know. And someone had their phone out." "The video." "You saw it?" "No. I saw it was on someone's story. I didn't—" I didn't watch it. I didn't want to watch it. "I saw the preview." "Right." She laughs. Not a real laugh. "Right. So you've seen—yeah. I just ran. Out the back door. Over the fence. That's where—" She holds up her hand. "I cut it on the top. The fence top. I didn't even feel it until I was in the street." "Your phone died?" "Phone died. I was going to just—keep walking. I don't know where I was going. But then I thought about the documentary. The girl. The one who waited three hours to call." "You borrowed someone's phone." "A man walking his dog. He looked terrified of me. I think I looked—yeah. He let me use it. I texted you." "You texted me three blood drops." "That was the code." "That was the code," I repeat. We sit with it. The cold is worse now. Or maybe it's the same and I've just stopped moving. My Geography textbook is still on my bed at home, open, glowing yellow. My mum's alarm will go off at 6:15. In six hours and—what time is it—I check my phone. 1:03am. In five hours and twelve minutes my mum's alarm will go off and she'll come in to wake me up and I won't be there. "Are you hurt anywhere else?" I say. "No." "Bea." "No. I'm not. I'm just—I'm fine. I'm fine. I just need to—" She looks at me. "My actual phone is still there. At Tom's." "Okay. We'll get it tomorrow." "No. You don't—" She pulls her hand away from mine, from my sleeve. "It has everything on it, Hatt. Messages. Photos. Screenshots—screenshots of things people have said about other people. If someone goes through it. If Tom—" "Okay." "I can't leave it there." "Okay." "It has everything on it." "I said okay." She looks at me. I look at her. The broken swing is moving slightly even though there's no wind. Or maybe there is wind and I just can't feel it because I'm cold everywhere already. "Right," I say. "Okay. Right."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 is a quietly accomplished piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and the established voice. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses carefully. VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Hattie's voice is well-maintained throughout. The internal monologue about the Geography textbook glowing yellow and the precise calculation of when her mum's alarm will go off (5 hours and 12 minutes) is exactly the kind of anxious, displaced fixation the plan called for. The prose is clipped, the humour is absent because she's genuinely scared, and the literalness of "Not a question. Just—say it" is pitch-perfect for the character profile. Bea's dialogue is fragmented and self-interrupting in the way the profile describes, and her focus on the shoe (mentioned briefly but pointedly) is consistent with the character note that she's "more upset about the shoe than the video, initially." The opening paragraph about the Year 8 incident with the boy who called Bea the n-word is a strong piece of characterisation—it establishes history, shows rather than tells the depth of the friendship, and does so without sentimentality. "That was a different kind of not-crying. This is worse." is one of the chapter's best lines: economical, emotionally precise, and entirely in Hattie's voice. DIALOGUE The dialogue is largely strong. Bea's account of the party ("I didn't want to be the Year 10 who couldn't handle it") is naturalistic and fragmented in the right way. The callback to the documentary code is handled well—it comes in organically rather than being explained in a neat paragraph, as the plan specified. The repeated "okay" exchange near the end is effective: it shows Hattie processing, committing, without any articulate emotional declaration. One slight weakness: "You texted me three blood drops" / "That was the code" / "That was the code" feels slightly too neat as a beat—it's trying to be wry but lands a little flat, like the chapter is winking at itself. It's a minor issue. SHOWING VS TELLING The chapter is almost entirely show-don't-tell. The description of Bea—mascara, loose braids, the sick on the sleeve—is specific and sensory without being overwrought. The detail of Hattie pulling her sleeve down to press against Bea's palm is a small, physical act of care that communicates the friendship without stating it. The broken swing moving "slightly even though there's no wind" is the chapter's one moment of mild atmospheric reach, and it works because it's grounded in Hattie's uncertainty ("Or maybe there is wind and I just can't feel it because I'm cold everywhere already"). FORCED POETRY / PURPLE PROSE The prose is admirably restrained. The "dead arm" simile for the broken chain is the most overtly metaphorical moment, and it's appropriate—it's the kind of slightly grim, unpoetic comparison a 14-year-old would make. There's no purple prose. The writing doesn't strain for effect. PLOT AND CHAPTER PLAN ADHERENCE The chapter follows its plan closely: Hattie finds Bea, Bea explains in fragments, the cut hand is established, the cliffhanger of "it has everything on it" is delivered. The plan specified that Hattie should think about the Geography mock—this is present (the textbook glowing yellow). The plan said Hattie's instinct is to minimise but she can tell it won't be fine—this is handled subtly through her not saying "it's fine" and just saying "okay" repeatedly. The chapter ends on the right note. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT The chapter is genuinely moving without being sentimental. The Year 8 flashback earns its place. The physical care (sleeve pressed to palm) is affecting. The ending—two girls in a cold park, the broken swing, the repeated "okay"—has the right weight. WEAKNESSES The chapter is competent to excellent throughout but rarely transcends into something truly surprising. The beats are well-executed but largely expected given the setup. The "dead arm" simile is the only moment of real creative risk, and it's a small one. The chapter does exactly what it sets out to do, which is both its strength and its slight limitation—there's no moment that genuinely startles or reframes. The "Right. Okay. Right." ending is good but slightly predictable as a closing rhythm for this kind of chapter. The dog barking and stopping is a small atmospheric touch that works. The man walking his dog being "terrified" of Bea is a good detail that implies something about how Bea looked without stating it. Overall: a well-crafted chapter that executes its plan faithfully, maintains voice consistently, and earns its emotional moments without sentimentality. It's not a masterpiece—it lacks the occasional flash of genuine surprise—but it's genuinely good YA fiction. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4: Bright Spaces The Tesco Extra is on the retail park. It takes us eleven minutes to walk there because Bea is barefoot on one foot and keeps stopping to pull things out of her sole. I give her my left trainer. It's too big for her. She doesn't say thank you. I don't expect her to. The Tesco is the big kind—the kind that sells everything, not just food. The kind that's too bright, like being inside a hospital, or a spaceship. The automatic doors open and the light hits us and I watch Bea flinch. She looks worse in here. The fluorescents don't let you hide. Her mascara is two black lines down her face. The sick on her sleeve is visible. A man in a hi-vis vest by the reductions aisle looks at us and then looks away. People always look away. I get a basket. I don't know why. There's nothing we need from Tesco except to be somewhere warm. But the basket feels like purpose. We walk through the aisles—cereal, pasta, baking—and I put Freddos in the basket because they're 25p and because I need to do something with my hands. Bea doesn't pick anything up. She's walking with her arms crossed, holding herself. The tea machine is by the bakery section. I buy two teas. They're £1.30 each and they taste like hot water with ambitions but Bea wraps both hands around the cup and I can see her fingers stop shaking. We sit on the bench outside. The air is cold but the tea is warm and we're in that strange zone—outside Tesco at 1:30am, a girl with no shoes and a girl with one shoe, drinking tea. It's so normal it's stupid. "I need to see it," I say. "See what." "The video. The Snapchat story." Bea doesn't say anything. I take out my phone. 19%. I open Snapchat. The story is from someone called @jakesmithyy—I don't know him, he must be sixth form. The story is fourteen snaps long. Most of them are blurry—music, bottles, people dancing. I tap through. Snap one: a girl laughing. Snap two: the kitchen, bottles everywhere. Snap three: Tom L with his arm around someone. Snap four—six seconds. Bea, in the kitchen. Bent over. The sound of laughter. The caption: "lightweight 💀." I don't watch it again. I don't need to. Six seconds. That's all it is. Six seconds of Bea being sick in a kitchen and people laughing. That's all it takes. I show Bea. She holds my phone. She watches it once. Her face doesn't change. She hands it back. "Right," she says. "We need to get your phone." "Hattie—" "You said it has everything on it. Messages. Screenshots. So we need to get it. Where's Tom's house?" "You can't just go there." "Why not?" "Because it's—there are people there. Or there were. And you're—" "I'm what." She looks at me. "You're you." I don't know what that means. I think it means I'm not the kind of person who walks into a sixth-form party and demands things. She's right. I'm not. But she's sitting here with sick on her top and a cut on her hand and one shoe and I'm all she's got. "Give me the address," I say. "Hattie." "Bea. Give me the address." She tells me. Oakmere Close. It's on the new-build estate, the one I was just at. The one where all the houses look the same. She tells me it's the one with the blue door. She doesn't have a house number. She says it doesn't matter because the plant pot outside is smashed—someone knocked it over during the party. "Stay here," I say. "Where am I going to go." "Buy more tea. Here." I give her £3 in coins from my pocket. "Don't move." "I'm not going to move." "Bea." "I said I'm not going to move." I leave her on the bench. I walk back toward the estate. The retail park is empty behind me—just orange lights and the trolley that's still by the kerb. Ahead, the new-build houses are dark, all of them, except one. Oakmere Close. Blue door. I can see it from the end of the street. There's a light on upstairs.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. The writing follows the chapter plan closely: Tesco Extra as a bright, warm refuge, the Freddos, the tea machine, the video reveal, the argument about the phone, and Hattie leaving Bea on the bench with £3. The cliffhanger—the light on upstairs—lands cleanly. The voice is the chapter's strongest asset. Hattie's internal logic is consistent and specific: the basket as "purpose," the Freddos because she needs something to do with her hands, the tea that "tastes like hot water with ambitions." That last line is the chapter's best—it's exactly the kind of unpoetic, slightly deflating simile a tired 14-year-old would produce, and it earns its small comedy without straining. The prose is appropriately clipped. When Hattie is scared, the sentences get shorter. The rhythm is controlled. The character work is strong. Bea's silence when she watches the video—"Her face doesn't change"—is more devastating than any articulated reaction would be. The exchange "You're you" / "I don't know what that means" is quietly excellent: it captures the social gap between them without spelling it out, and Hattie's self-awareness ("I think it means I'm not the kind of person who walks into a sixth-form party and demands things") is honest without being self-pitying. The detail of Hattie giving Bea her left trainer and Bea not saying thank you is exactly right—it shows the texture of their friendship without commentary. The video description is handled well. Six seconds. The caption. Hattie doesn't watch it again. The restraint here is correct and effective. The chapter doesn't linger or sentimentalise. The dialogue is mostly strong. The repetition in "I'm not going to move" / "Bea" / "I said I'm not going to move" captures the exhausted, slightly fractious dynamic between them. The "Give me the address" exchange is terse and purposeful. Some minor weaknesses: "like being inside a hospital, or a spaceship" is a slightly weak double simile—the hospital comparison is apt, the spaceship less so, and the "or" makes it feel uncertain rather than deliberately comic. The observation "People always look away" is a small moment of generalisation that edges toward telling rather than showing. The final image—"There's a light on upstairs"—is effective but not surprising; it's exactly what the plan called for and it delivers without exceeding expectations. The chapter is perhaps slightly thin on the comic darkness the plan promises. The Tesco scene is well-observed but the comedy is muted—which may be appropriate given the emotional weight of the video reveal, but the plan's promise of "dark comedy that grows from genuine distress" is only partially fulfilled here. The chapter is more genuinely distressing than darkly comic, which isn't a failure but is a slight imbalance. Overall, this is confident, controlled writing that serves its characters and its plan. It doesn't overreach, doesn't sentimentalise, and doesn't explain. The voice is consistent and the emotional beats land without being forced. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5: Front Door I stand on the path for a full minute. The plant pot is smashed, just like Bea said—terracotta bits all over the paving, soil in a dark pile. Someone's heel print is in the soil. The blue door has a glass panel and through it I can see a hallway. A coat rack. A shoe. Just one shoe, just lying there. I think about Bea's other Joules trainer, the one she lost, and whether it's somewhere in this house. I don't have a plan. I want to be clear about that. I'm standing here at 1:50am in one trainer and one sock because I gave Bea my other one, and I have no plan. I considered going around the back. I considered knocking. I considered leaving. I considered all three in a loop for sixty seconds and then I knocked because I couldn't stand there anymore. Nothing happens. I knock again, harder. My knuckles hurt. The door is solid—new build, everything solid but cheap, like it's pretending to be a proper house. Footsteps. Someone coming downstairs. The light in the hallway clicks on. The door opens. A boy. Tall. Not Tom L—I don't know who this is. Dark hair, the messy kind that boys spend time on. He's wearing a T-shirt and joggers and he looks annoyed and sleepy and confused. "Yeah?" "Hi. Um. I'm looking for my—I'm looking for someone. My cousin. She was at the party. Here. Earlier." He blinks. "Party's over." "Yeah. I know. She—she left her phone." He looks at me. I look at him. I'm aware of what I look like: fourteen, one trainer, one sock, hoodie, no coat. I look exactly like what I am, which is a girl who has no business being here. "Hold on," he says. He goes back upstairs. I hear him moving around. I stand in the doorway. The house smells like beer and something burned—like someone tried to cook and gave up. There are cups everywhere. The kitchen is visible from the hallway—a mess, cans on the counter, something dark on the floor that I don't want to identify. I should not be in this house. He comes back. He's holding a phone. Not Bea's. It's a Samsung. Bea has an iPhone. "No, that's not—different one. It's an iPhone. Black case. She had a—there's a sticker on the back. A ladybird." He stares at me. "There's one on the counter." "In the kitchen?" "Yeah." "Can I—" I don't wait. I step past him. The kitchen is worse from inside. There's a stain on the floor by the bin and I think about Bea bent over and I feel it in my stomach. The phone is on the counter, next to a half-empty bottle of vodka. Black case. Ladybird sticker. Cracked screen, cracked worse on the bottom left. I pick it up. It's dead. "Cheers," I say, to no one, because the boy is still in the hallway. I walk back through. A girl is at the top of the stairs now. She's wrapped in a blanket. She looks at me like I'm a problem. "Who's that?" she says. "Wrong house," I say. I leave. I walk fast. I don't run because running is suspicious. I walk fast with Bea's phone in my hoodie pocket and my one trainer slapping the pavement. I'm halfway down the street when I see the police car. It turns onto Oakmere Close. Not fast, no sirens, just the headlights sweeping across the identical houses. I don't know if it's coming for me. I don't know if someone called them. Maybe the girl in the blanket. Maybe the boy. Maybe someone else, someone who saw a fourteen-year-old girl in one shoe walking into a house at 2am and thought: that's not right. I turn the corner. I press myself against a fence. The police car passes. It doesn't stop. I'm shaking. Not cold shaking—proper shaking, the kind where your teeth go. I can feel Bea's phone against my hip through the pocket. I did it. I got the phone. I don't feel like I did it. I feel like something happened to me and I'm only now catching up. I think about my Geography mock. Coastal erosion. I can't remember any of it. I can't remember a single thing about coastal erosion. I think about my mum. Her mug on the draining board. The note on the fridge. I walk back toward Tesco. My sock is wet through. I don't care. I did it. I got the phone. I just have to get back to Bea and then we'll—we'll figure out the rest. We'll get the bus. We'll go home. It'll be fine. It'll be fine. Bea is not on the bench. She's not on the bench and her tea cup is there, half full, and the £3 I gave her is in coins on the seat. I look left. I look right. The car park is empty. The automatic doors open and close for no one. My phone buzzes. A text from Bea. "Police here. Behind Tesco. Don't come."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and discipline. The model has followed the chapter outline closely—Hattie approaches the house, knocks, retrieves the phone through a combination of bluff and opportunism, and then faces the cliffhanger of the police car followed by Bea's warning text. The plan called for Hattie to be "shaking" after leaving, to think about the Geography mock, and to think about her mum—all of which appear in the text, integrated naturally rather than mechanically. The voice is the chapter's greatest strength. Hattie's narration is precisely calibrated: dry, specific, self-aware without being precocious. "I want to be clear about that" is a perfect Hattie construction—the kind of thing a teenager says to an imaginary audience when they're embarrassed about their own incompetence. The detail of giving Bea her trainer and walking in one sock is both funny and grounding, and the chapter earns it without over-explaining. The Geography mock intrusion ("Coastal erosion. I can't remember any of it. I can't remember a single thing about coastal erosion.") lands exactly as intended—the comedy of the mundane intruding on crisis, which is the story's central tonal register. The prose is admirably restrained. Short sentences when Hattie is scared. No overwriting. The description of the house—"new build, everything solid but cheap, like it's pretending to be a proper house"—is the kind of specific, slightly mean observation that feels genuinely teenage rather than performed. The smell of "beer and something burned" is sensory without being showy. The dialogue is minimal but functional. The boy's "Yeah?" and "Hold on" are appropriately flat. Hattie's "Wrong house" exit line is good—it's the kind of thing you say when you're panicking and it's both plausible and slightly absurd. The girl in the blanket is exactly what the plan called for: a minor character who's just there. The chapter's emotional architecture is sound. The shaking after leaving—"Not cold shaking—proper shaking, the kind where your teeth go"—is the moment where Hattie's bravado collapses into genuine fear, and the prose earns it by having been controlled up to that point. The "I did it. I got the phone. I don't feel like I did it. I feel like something happened to me and I'm only now catching up" is one of the chapter's best lines—it captures the dissociation of adrenaline without reaching for metaphor. The cliffhanger is well-constructed. The double beat—police car, then Bea's text—is more effective than a single shock. The coins on the bench are a good detail. Minor criticisms: The "I think about my mum. Her mug on the draining board. The note on the fridge" is slightly underdeveloped—it gestures at emotional weight without quite landing it. The note on the fridge hasn't been established in this chapter, so it reads as slightly opaque (though it may have been established earlier). The chapter is also perhaps slightly short on the comic texture that the plan promised—the "heist" is almost too smooth, too quick. The plan said "they have no plan and it shows," but Hattie's improvisation actually works fairly well, which slightly undercuts the intended incompetence. The boy is cooperative almost immediately, which reduces the comic friction. Overall, this is confident, disciplined work. The voice is consistent, the tone is controlled, the plan is followed faithfully, and the prose never overreaches. It's not a masterpiece—it lacks the occasional flash of genuine surprise or the deeper emotional resonance that would push it to the highest tier—but it's very good YA fiction executed with real craft. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6: Behind the Shed I go around Tesco. Of course I go around Tesco. Bea texts me "don't come" and I come. That's the whole thing about me, apparently. That's the whole thing. The back of Tesco is the loading bay—big shutters, bins, those yellow bollards. There's a path that goes along the side, and then a fence, and then a gap in the fence that leads to someone's garden. I find Bea behind a shed. She's crouched down with her knees up. She's got both my trainers now. I don't know where she found the other one. "I said don't come," she says. "I know." We crouch together. The shed smells like damp wood. There's a plant pot next to it—no, a bin, an old green bin with no lid. The garden belongs to one of the houses that backs onto the retail park. There's a light on in the upstairs window but no movement. "Was it the police?" I say. "A community officer. Woman. She was in the Tesco. She saw me and asked if I was okay and I said I was waiting for my mum and she said it was a bit late and I said my mum was on her way and she just—looked at me. And then she got on her radio and I just left." "Did she follow you?" "I don't know. I don't think so." We listen. Nothing. A dog somewhere. A car on the main road. The low hum of Tesco, which never stops, even at 2am. Fridges, I think. The fridges never stop. Bea's phone is still dead. I hold the button. Nothing. The screen stays black. "It's not going to turn on, Hattie." "I know." "So what's the point." "The point is they don't have it. They don't have your phone. Tom doesn't have it. Jake whatever-his-name-is doesn't have it. We have it." "They already have the video, though. They don't need the phone." "But they don't have your messages. Your photos. Your screenshots. That's something." Bea doesn't say anything. She pulls her knees tighter. The cut on her palm has started bleeding again, or maybe it never stopped. I can see it glistening. "You should put pressure on that," I say. "I don't care about my hand." "Well, you should." "Why?" "Because it's your hand, Bea." She laughs. That laugh again. Not real. "I want to go home." "Okay." "We will. In a minute. I just want to—" She puts her head on her knees. "I'm so tired." We sit. The cold gets into everything—my feet, my ears, the small of my back where my hoodie rides up. I think about Mum's kitchen. The green dishwasher light. Custard creams in the cupboard. "Can I say something?" Bea says. "Yeah." "I'm always the one things happen to." I don't say anything. "Like—always. I'm always the one who gets caught. Who gets filmed. Who gets in trouble. And people love it. They love watching it happen." "Bea—" "You don't get it." Her voice changes. Harder now. "You weren't there. You don't know what it's like when people are watching you and you can feel them deciding what you are. You're just—" She stops. "You're just there, Hattie. No one's looking at you like that. You don't know." I feel something shift in my chest. Something hot and ugly. "I literally snuck out for you," I say. "I didn't ask you to." "You texted me. You texted the code." "I was drunk. I was—" "What, so you didn't mean it?" "I didn't mean—Hattie, I didn't mean for you to come. I just needed to tell someone." "Tell someone. Not me specifically. Just someone." "That's not what I—" "No, I get it. I get it. I'm just the person who was awake. I'm just the person who answered." We're both quiet. She's right, somewhere in there. And I'm right, somewhere in there. And neither of us can find the right part. A fox walks along the bottom of the garden. It stops, looks at us—eyes catching the light—and keeps walking. We watch it go. "Your mum's going to kill you," Bea says. "Yeah." "Like, actually kill you." "Yeah." "I'll come to your funeral. What flowers do you want?" "I don't know. Cheap ones." She almost laughs. Almost. I almost laugh. Almost. My phone buzzes. Mum. "Where are you."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the plan and the established voice. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Character Consistency** Hattie's voice is well-maintained throughout. The opening gambit — "That's the whole thing about me, apparently. That's the whole thing." — is a confident, dry self-observation that feels genuinely teenage without being performed. The internal monologue is clipped and specific. The detail about the fridges ("The fridges never stop") is exactly the kind of odd, exhausted fixation the plan called for. The Geography mock doesn't appear here, but the kitchen detail (green dishwasher light, custard creams) substitutes effectively as a comfort-object thought. Hattie's voice gets shorter and plainer when scared, as planned. Bea's dialogue is consistent with her profile — the lashing out, the "you're just there, Hattie," the pivot to dark humour about the funeral. The funeral joke lands because it's been seeded in the character profiles and it arrives at exactly the right moment of emotional exhaustion. It's earned. **The Argument** The argument in the shed is the chapter's centrepiece and it's handled with real skill. Neither character is entirely right or wrong. Bea's "I didn't mean for you to come. I just needed to tell someone" is genuinely painful — it's the kind of thing that's true and cruel simultaneously, and the model doesn't soften it. Hattie's response ("Not me specifically. Just someone") is the kind of hurt that doesn't announce itself as hurt. This is showing rather than telling at its best. The plan called for "two people who've said too much, sitting in cold grass" and that's exactly what's delivered. **Sensory Detail** The chapter is grounded in specific, unglamorous sensory detail: the loading bay, yellow bollards, the damp shed smell, the green bin with no lid, the Tesco hum. These details are functional rather than decorative, which is right for Hattie's voice. The fox is a nice touch — it appears in the character notes as a fear object, and here it's deployed as a moment of shared absurdity that breaks the tension without resolving it. **Dialogue** The dialogue is largely excellent. It's fragmented, realistic, and carries subtext. "Because it's your hand, Bea." / "Why?" / "Because it's your hand, Bea." — this exchange is quietly funny and also tender. The repetition works. The argument escalates naturally and stops at the right moment, without resolution. **Weaknesses** A few minor issues. "Something hot and ugly" when Hattie feels the shift in her chest is slightly generic — the plan called for minimal metaphor and this is one of the weaker moments where the prose reaches for something it doesn't quite find. The "eyes catching the light" fox description is a small lurch toward the poetic that doesn't quite fit Hattie's voice. These are minor. The chapter is perhaps slightly short on the kind of comic timing the plan promised — the humour is present but muted, which may be appropriate given the emotional weight of the argument, but the plan's promise of "dark comedy that grows from genuine distress" is more distress than comedy here. **Plan Adherence** The chapter follows the plan closely: hiding behind the shed, the argument about visibility, Bea's "you're just there," Hattie's "I literally snuck out for you," the almost-laugh, the mum text. The plan's cliffhanger is delivered cleanly. The detail about Bea having both trainers is a nice addition not in the plan — it's the kind of small, unexplained detail that feels real. **Overall** This is confident, controlled writing that earns its emotional moments without sentimentalising them. The dialogue is the strongest element. The prose is appropriately plain. The chapter does what it sets out to do. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7: Kitchen Light We walk home. It takes forty minutes because Bea can't run and I can't make myself. We don't talk. We walk past the retail park, past the closed gym, past the bus stop where I caught the N29 three hours ago. The sky is doing that thing—not light yet, but not properly dark. Like it can't decide. Like it's waiting for something too. My phone has seventeen missed calls. Four texts. "Where are you." "Answer your phone." "This isn't funny." "I'm calling the police." I don't reply to any of them. I can't. What would I say? We turn onto my street. Everything is quiet. All the lights are off. All except one. Our kitchen window. It's on, and I can see—movement. Shape. Someone in the kitchen. "She's awake," I say. Bea stops walking. "I can't go in there." "You have to." "I can't. I look—I can't." "Bea. She already knows I'm gone. She's been calling me for an hour. I have to go in." "What about me?" "Come in with me." "And say what?" We stand on the pavement. My house is twelve doors down. The kitchen light is a yellow rectangle in the dark. I can see Mum's shadow. She's not sitting. She's standing. She's probably been standing for hours. "Just—come in. We'll deal with it." We walk to the door. I could use my key but I knock instead. I don't know why. It feels like the kind of thing you knock for. Mum opens the door. She's still in her work clothes—the black trousers, the blue top. Her reading glasses are on her head. Her face does something I can't describe. Not angry. Not relieved. Something before both. "Right," she says. She looks at me. She looks at Bea. She looks at Bea's face, the mascara, the missing shoe, the blood on her hand. She looks at me again. "Right," she says again. "Come in. Sit down. No—sit down. Don't move." We sit at the kitchen table. The light is too bright, same as Tesco. Mum gets the first aid box from under the sink. It's a plastic one with a red cross on it. She opens it, takes out an antiseptic wipe, holds Bea's hand, cleans the cut. Bea winces. Mum doesn't say sorry. "When did you do this?" Mum says. "Earlier," Bea says. "How?" "Fence." Mum puts a plaster on. She gets another one. Two plasters, side by side. Then she puts the box away and fills the kettle. We sit there. The kettle boils. It's loud. Everything is loud in this kitchen. "Are you hurt anywhere else?" Mum says. She's looking at Bea. "No." "Have you been drinking?" Bea's voice is very small. "I was." "Whose party?" Bea doesn't answer. Mum looks at me. I don't answer either. "Right," Mum says. She takes out her phone. "I'm calling your mum, Bea." Bea doesn't react. Mum walks into the hallway. I hear her voice—low, serious. "Yes, she's here. She's okay. No, she's—yes. Can you come? She's—yes. Okay." She comes back. Pours tea. Puts a mug in front of each of us. Puts the milk away. Wipes the counter. She's doing the things she does when she doesn't know what to do—tasks, small tasks, things with steps. "I thought something had happened to you," she says. She's not looking at me. She's looking at the counter she just wiped. "I came in to check on you and you weren't there. Your bed was—I thought something had happened." "Mum—" "I was sat here. I called you and called you and you didn't—I thought—" Her voice cracks. Just there, at the end. She stops. Picks up her mug. Puts it down again without drinking. Bea's mum arrives in eleven minutes. The doorbell rings and Bea stands up. Her mum is in a coat over pyjamas. She's tall, taller than Bea. She doesn't shout. She looks at Bea and her face does the same thing Mum's face did. That thing before. "Let's go home," she says. Bea follows her to the door. She turns around. She looks at me. She doesn't say anything. I don't say anything. She leaves. Mum and I are in the kitchen. The clock says 3:47. "We'll talk in the morning," she says. It is the morning. We both know that.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is a quiet, controlled piece of writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. The model has understood the assignment: this is not a chapter of catharsis or resolution, but of aftermath, and it renders that aftermath with considerable restraint and skill. The prose is appropriately stripped back. When Hattie is scared and exhausted, the sentences get shorter and plainer, exactly as the style guide demanded. "We walk home. It takes forty minutes because Bea can't run and I can't make myself." That second clause—"I can't make myself"—is doing real work without announcing itself. It suggests emotional paralysis without explaining it. This is the chapter's consistent strength: it trusts the reader. The sky description ("Like it can't decide. Like it's waiting for something too.") is the chapter's most overtly "poetic" moment, and it's a mild offender—slightly too neat as a pathetic fallacy, slightly too self-aware for Hattie's stated voice. But it's brief and doesn't recur, so it doesn't damage the chapter significantly. The four texts from Mum are perfectly chosen. The escalation from flat statement to panic to threat is economical and characterful. "This isn't funny" is particularly good—it implies Mum has considered the possibility that this is a prank, which is a very specific parental thought. Mum's characterisation is excellent and consistent with the profile. The "Right" repeated twice, the first aid box, the wiping of the counter she just wiped—these are all exactly the behaviours described: tasks as displacement, love expressed through function. "She's doing the things she does when she doesn't know what to do—tasks, small tasks, things with steps." This line is slightly tell-don't-show (Hattie is explaining what we've just seen), but it's in Hattie's voice and feels like the kind of thing a tired, observant teenager would articulate to herself, so it earns partial forgiveness. The voice crack in Mum's dialogue is well-placed. "I thought—" trailing off is more affecting than any completed sentence would be. The model resists the urge to give Mum a speech, which is exactly right. Bea's departure is handled with real skill. "She looks at me. She doesn't say anything. I don't say anything. She leaves." This is the chapter's best moment—the friendship's complexity, the unresolved shed argument, the gratitude that can't be spoken, all compressed into three short sentences. It's genuinely moving without being sentimental. The ending is the chapter's other strong moment. "We'll talk in the morning." / "It is the morning." / "We both know that." This is clean, earned, and funny in the dark way the prompt demands. It doesn't over-explain. The comedy is in the rhythm, not the construction, exactly as planned. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the walk home, the kitchen, Mum's controlled response, the first aid, the tea, the phone call, Bea's departure, the final exchange. Nothing is missed, nothing is added unnecessarily. Where the chapter is slightly weaker: Hattie's internal voice is somewhat muted compared to the profile's promise. The Geography mock doesn't appear (the plan specified it would), and while its absence isn't a fatal flaw, it was meant to be a recurring comic beat that grounds Hattie's character. The chapter is perhaps slightly too clean—the prose is controlled to the point where Hattie's characteristic nervous repetition ("like," the lists, the catastrophising) is largely absent. She's more composed here than the profile suggests she'd be. This may be intentional—she's past the crisis point—but it means the chapter loses some of the voice's distinctiveness. The dialogue is generally strong. "Fence." as Bea's answer to "How?" is excellent. The Bea-Hattie exchange on the pavement ("And say what?") is natural and unforced. Mum's dialogue is pitch-perfect. Overall, this is a confident, restrained chapter that earns its emotional moments without sentimentalising them. It's not flashy, but it's doing exactly what it should be doing, and doing it well. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8: Monday The bus smells like wet coat. It always smells like wet coat on Mondays but today it's worse because it rained all weekend and everyone's coat is still damp. I'm sitting upstairs at the back. My phone is on 47% because I actually charged it last night, which feels like the only normal thing I've done in three days. I haven't seen Bea since Friday night. Saturday was—Mum didn't shout. She didn't do the silence thing either. She made breakfast and we ate it and she said "we need to talk about this" and I said "okay" and she said "not now, I need to think" and that was it. Sunday she cleaned the whole house. I helped. We didn't talk about it. She made a roast. She always makes a roast on Sunday. Ollie talked about Minecraft for forty-five minutes straight and nobody stopped him. This morning there's a packed lunch on the side. There's been one every day since Thursday. With a note. Today's says "Good luck with the week, love you, Mum x" which is Mum's way of saying things she can't say out loud. I get to school at 8:35. Form room. Priya is already there. "Good weekend?" she says. She doesn't know. Why would she know? I wasn't at the party. I wasn't in the video. I was behind a shed and then in a kitchen and then in my bed pretending to sleep. "Yeah," I say. "Fine." I sit down. I open my bag. I take out my planner. I stare at the page. Nothing goes in. The page stays blank. I'm waiting for it. The whispers. The phones. The thing where someone looks at you and then looks away too fast. I'm waiting for Jake whatever-his-name-is to have sent the video to everyone. I'm waiting for the whole school to know that Bea was sick in a kitchen at a sixth-form party and someone filmed it and it was on Snapchat for twenty-four hours. Nothing happens. Nobody says anything. Priya asks if I've done the Maths homework. I haven't. She lets me copy hers. Registration starts. Mr. Harrison reads out notices. Someone's lost a water bottle. There's a chess club on Wednesday. Bea isn't in form. I find her at her locker at break. She's standing there, getting books out. She looks—normal. She's wearing the gold hoops. Her braids are neat. She has both shoes. She looks tired, but you'd only notice if you knew. "Hey," I say. "Hey." We stand there. Someone walks past—Jake, actually, Jake Smith, the one whose story it was. He doesn't look at us. He's talking to someone about football. "Has anyone—" I start. "A few people. Not many. I don't think—Jake's story expired. I don't know if anyone saved it." "But—" "Someone might have. I don't know. I can't—" She closes her locker. "I can't spend every day wondering if someone's going to bring it up. So I'm just going to—go to lessons. And do the thing." "The thing?" "The thing where you pretend nothing happened." Right. We walk to assembly together. The hall smells like floor cleaner. Mrs. Allen talks about litter. Someone's been leaving crisp packets in the science block. This is apparently important enough for a whole assembly. I sit next to Bea. Our shoulders are touching. She doesn't move away. The assembly ends. We file out. Jake Smith walks past us again. He does the thing—being sick, fake gagging, laughing. His mate laughs. Bea looks at him. Just looks. She doesn't say anything. Jake stops laughing. He walks faster. "That was good," I say. "I didn't do anything." "I know. That's why it was good." At lunch we sit in the canteen. Bea gets chips. I have the packed lunch. KitKat, cheese sandwich, apple, note. I put the note in my pocket without reading it again. Bea reaches over and takes a crisp from my packet. She does this every lunchtime. She's done it since Year 7. "Geography mock's Friday," she says. "I haven't revised." "Same." "I don't even remember what coastal erosion is." "Something about cliffs." "Yeah." We eat. The canteen is loud. Someone drops a tray. Someone laughs. Someone else says "that's so dead." Normal sounds. Monday sounds. My phone buzzes. A text from Bea. She's sitting across from me.
